topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a sickening thud. Subject line: "Your piece, 'Echoes in the Concrete'." My stomach immediately tightened into a knot, a cold, hard thing. I clicked it open, and the words swam before my eyes. The article, my meticulously crafted analysis of urban decay, was there, undeniably mine, yet the byline read: "By Julian Vance." Julian Vance. A name I didn't recognize, a ghost in the machine of my own achievement. I reread the text, searching for any trace of my unique phrasing, any signature thought. Nothing. It was a perfect, chilling forgery. I started pacing the small apartment, the floorboards groaning under my frantic steps. Every shadow seemed to shift, every distant siren sounded like a deliberate surveillance. I checked my computer screen repeatedly, a nervous twitch in my jaw. Who had access? Was this a prank? Or worse, was someone meticulously dismantling my credibility, piece by careful piece? I began systematically reviewing every digital footprint I had, feeling the need to map out every possible avenue of infiltration, my breath catching shallowly in my chest with every unanswered question.

[story 2]

I stared at the printout, the ink mocking me. The headline screamed my topic, the arguments resonated with my own voice, but the attribution was alien. "Authored by Marcus Thorne." Marcus Thorne. The realization hit me like a physical blow, stealing the air from my lungs. My hands started trembling, the paper rattling in my grip. I dropped it onto the desk, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet room. I stood up abruptly, my eyes darting towards the window, assessing the street below as if expecting someone to be watching from behind the blinds. Every mundane sound—the hum of the refrigerator, the distant traffic—seemed amplified, charged with hidden meaning. I began to check my phone, scrolling through old correspondence, searching for any hint of a leak, a compromised account. My pulse hammered against my ribs, a frantic drumbeat demanding immediate action. If someone could steal my voice, what else could they access? I started locking the doors, testing the deadbolt, the click of the lock sounding unnervingly final.

[story 3]

When the notification popped up, the world tilted. My carefully constructed sense of ownership dissolved instantly. The article, the culmination of months of solitary effort, was presented under another identity. "Submitted by Eleanor Reed." Eleanor Reed. The name felt like a deliberate insult, a calculated theft of my intellectual property. A wave of intense agitation washed over me, making my skin prickle. I paced the length of the living room, my movements jerky and too fast. I kept glancing over my shoulder, convinced the hallway was suddenly occupied by unseen observers. My throat felt tight, constricted, as if I were struggling to draw a full breath. I picked up my phone, not to check social media, but to meticulously review the metadata of the publication link, hunting for any digital breadcrumbs left by the impostor. Each search query felt like an act of desperate defense, a frantic attempt to trace the invisible hand that had rewritten my reality. The silence of the room was deafening, punctuated only by the rapid, uneven rhythm of my own heartbeat.
